data.title: Power Solutions: This Painful Dip Looks Like A Gift
data.description:
PSIX Stock Went On Sale

Power Solutions (PSIX), which is a small-cap power systems company that operates in the US and internationally, went from $2/share to over $115/share in about a year, being one of the best-performing stocks in the world for the given period. But in the past few months, it has sold off by over 54%, and it keeps testing new price levels below the 200-day moving average:

The stock is cratering on insider selling and Q3 margin fears, but from what I see, PSIX is overdelivering on revenues and earnings, so I believe PSIX moved from deep value to something closer to a fairly discounted structural grower. There might not be massive top and bottom line expansions ahead, but the growth should stay in place, so PSIX has become reasonably valued after the repricing that took place lately. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating coverage on the stock today.

My Updated Reasoning on PSIX

When I looked at PSIX last time, the company's sales and EPS were up 42% and 168% YoY, respectively (for Q1 2025). Now, we can see that for Q3 2025, the growth hasn't cooled. On the contrary, the sales surged by a bigger YoY figure (+62%, to $203.8 million), and although the company lost 500 bps on the gross margin level, the diluted EPS was up 60% YoY ($1.20 vs. $0.75). The headline figures were much better than consensus, and it drove up estimates for Q4:

So, the Q3 print wasn't weak, in my view. Both revenue and EPS growth rates were above 50% YoY, and EBITDA dollars and EPS grew faster than revenue, creating a solid operating leverage at a larger scale. The whole margin debate, which is likely the main correction driver right now, boils down to where within Power Systems the growth is coming from (plus the cost of rapidly ramping capacity). The management noted in the MD&A section of the latest 10-Q that the 500 bps GP margin decline was due to "strong sales growth in comparatively lower‑margin products and temporary inefficiencies related to the accelerated production ramp‑up."

Within Power Systems, DC and certain alternative‑fuel products carry structurally higher margins. But in Q3, we saw a disproportionate revenue surge in lower‑margin SKUs (legacy standby generators and more commoditised power equipment). It leads me to a thought that the Q3 margin weakness we've seen was mainly mix- and ramp‑driven, not a structural business deterioration. On a YTD basis, when we take the last 9 months of data, we see that PSIX's gross margin is 26.9% (vs. 29.4% last year), and their EBITDA margin is even better than last year (17% vs. 16.7%). The ongoing shift toward higher‑growth DCs and away from mature industrial/transportation markets really accelerated in 2025.

This shift in markets reflects the Company's conscious strategic prioritization toward higher growth, higher‑margin markets with less emphasis on more mature markets.

Source: From PSIX's 10-Q

So it seems to me that PSIX is intentionally trying to sacrifice lower‑margin industrial volume to free up capacity and capital for data centers and O&G, so PSIX is effectively becoming a mission‑critical supplier to AI‑driven data center infrastructure, not just an industrial play. Once the mix shifts to higher‑margin data‑center SKUs, and as volumes scale, the Q3 inefficiencies should reverse, with gross margin recovering amid rising volumes on an already‑built cost base. At least that's my view on the current landscape. In other words, I'd put it this way: PSIX's Q3 margin compression was the cost of scaling in a surging end market, and its business model isn't failing so far.

Plus, on the balance sheet, we've seen total debt declining from $120 million (end of 2024) to only $96.7 million as of Q3, and the interest expense declined by ~44% YoY. PSIX has fully repaid the Weichai shareholder loan, now primarily relying on a syndicated bank revolver. I don't see risks to their financial conditions at this point.

I believe that once the margin compression steps aside and PSIX's operating leverage stays in, the market will be willing to grant it a higher multiple, just like it did in mid-2025. Right now, PSIX is trading at only 10x forward earnings, which is 3x lower than the TTM P/E multiple in August 2025.

We should keep in mind here that the consensus views the EPS growth next year at 22.87%, so the effective forward PEG ratio is close to ~0.52x.

Assuming that PSIX beats the EPS consensus by 10-15% over the next few quarters, thanks to its operating leverage and diminishing role of the Q3 headwinds, I think we may see $5.13 in EPS next year. At a P/E of 15x, the price target will be ~$76.95 per share. The upside is close to 46%, so that's why PSIX deserves to be rated as a "Buy" right now.

Risks

We need to see at least some evidence of a QoQ gross margin recovery (even by 100 or 200 bps) over the next few quarters for my bullish thesis to start working and the overall pessimism on the market to fade. Plus, the management needs to confirm that the share of higher‑margin data‑center SKUs within Power Systems is rising indeed - in this case, the investments they've made would become justified. There's a risk that these assumptions are not realized on a timely basis, and then PSIX stock will be hammered once again. Also, the stock seems to be in a price channel now, with a very broad width. I've shown the price chart in the very beginning, but if we zoom in, we'll see that the nearest support is 15-16% below the current price level. So, PSIX may continue its drawdown until it reaches that level again (and there's a risk that it can dive deeper than that).

Your Takeaway

From what I see, the risks are there, but at the same time, PSIX's revenue growth remains extraordinary and is driven by the right end markets (AI/data centers and higher‑spec power systems). Margins took a hit for the right reasons (i.e., mix and ramp‑up), while EBITDA, EPS, and balance‑sheet metrics all improved YoY - there's little to worry about at this point, in my understanding. Right now, PSIX is still valued like a somewhat cyclical industrial, but it's actively shifting toward cyclical-proof areas, and I think it can be undervalued by about 45-46% in the base case scenario. Given all that, I decided to give PSIX another "Buy" rating after its massive dip since mid-2025.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: ADP National Employment Report: 32K Private Jobs Lost In November
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Typically, the most anticipated economic news of the first week of a new month is Friday's jobs report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS). This monthly report contains a wealth of data for economists, the most publicized being the month-over-month change in total nonfarm employment.

However, with the government agencies still playing catch-up due to the shutdown, this crucial report will likely be delayed until later in the month, shifting the spotlight to the ADP National Employment Report as the primary jobs indicator for the week.

The ADP employment report revealed that 32,000 nonfarm private jobs were lost in November. This figure was below the expected addition of 5,000 jobs and marks a sharp decline from the 47,000 jobs added in October. The 32,000 loss is the largest since March 2023 and the fourth month of job losses in the past six months.

Here is a visualization of the two series over the past twelve months. There is no correlation between the ADP and BLS employment reports.

Here is an excerpt from today's ADP report press release:

"Hiring has been choppy of late as employers weather cautious consumers and an uncertain macroeconomic environment. And while November's slowdown was broad-based, it was led by a pullback among small businesses."

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in the ADP headline number since the company's earliest published data with the new methodology in 2010. This is quite a volatile series, so we've plotted the monthly data points as dots along with a six-month moving average, which gives us a clearer sense of the trend.

The six-month moving average has been slowing for nine straight months and currently sits at 11,000, its lowest level since the aftermath of the 2020 pandemic.

As we see in the chart above, the trend peaked in September 2015 and then went negative for the first time in late 2019, just before the NBER declared a recession start.

The COVID-19 pandemic has brought employment numbers down to levels we have never seen this century. The trend reached a new high in 2021 at 875,000 and has recently dropped back to pre-pandemic levels.

ADP Employment: Industry

The ADP report divides total nonfarm private employment into two categories: goods producing and service providing. Given that the US is primarily a services economy, it's no surprise that services employment has historically driven job growth. This reliance also explains why the sector was hit hardest when the pandemic brought many service-producing jobs to a halt.

In November, goods-producing jobs fell by 19,000, while service-providing jobs decreased by 12,000. The sector with the largest growth in November was education and health services, with a gain of 33,000. The biggest loser last month was professional and business services, with a loss of 26,000 jobs.

The next chart is a linear representation of each industry's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Region

ADP also gives us a breakdown of total nonfarm private employment into nine regions (census divisions). The region with the largest growth in November was the Pacific region, with a gain of 54,000 private jobs. Meanwhile, the South Atlantic region experienced the largest loss at -79,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each region's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Establishment Size

ADP also gives us a breakdown of total nonfarm private employment into five sizes. Establishments with 500+ employees added the most jobs in November, with a gain of 39,000. Meanwhile, small-mid size establishments with 20-49 employees experienced the largest loss at -74,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each establishment size's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Uniswap Reclaims $6.20 As Sharp Rotation Revives Strength
data.description:
By Ashutosh Sureka

Uniswap (UNI-USD) is trading at $6.07, up 10.7% in the past 24 hours, extending its rebound after a sharp recovery from the mid-$5.40 zone. The market cap stands at $3.82 billion, while daily trading volume at $498.52 million reflects heavy participation as buyers returned aggressively after yesterday’s capitulation drop. The day’s range between $5.39 and $6.17 highlights strong intraday volatility as UNI stabilizes above a key short-term resistance band.

Technical structure analysis

UNI remains below major EMAs on higher timeframes, but short-term structure shows clear improvement. The 20 EMA sits near $5.87, the 50 EMA near $5.93, the 100 EMA around $6.02, and the 200 EMA close to $6.03.

Price has reclaimed the faster EMAs and is now pressing directly into the heavier 100 and 200 EMAs, signalling early trend repair though not yet a confirmed breakout.

RSI on the 1-hour chart sits near 66.63 after an earlier spike toward 79.06, showing strong momentum that has cooled but remains constructive. UNI needs a decisive break above $6.20 for continuation, while slipping below $5.85 risks reopening downside toward last week’s lows.

On-chain flows analysis

On-chain flows have turned mildly positive after weeks of persistent red prints. Coinglass data shows a net inflow of about $762.17k on 3 December, marking one of the strongest green spikes in recent sessions.

This follows a long period of heavy outflows throughout October and November, during which market cap compression mirrored retreating sentiment. The latest stabilization in market cap suggests selective rotation back into DeFi despite overall caution.

Sentiment and fundamentals

UNI’s broader tone has improved as DeFi assets staged a sector-wide rebound. Liquidity remains thin, but whale accumulation has supported the recovery. However, strong overhead supply between $6.15 and $6.30 continues to cap momentum. Protocol-level developments have not yet sparked a major shift in behaviour, meaning sustained progress still depends on broader market tone and improved flow dynamics.

Short-term outlook

UNI has shown a strong intraday recovery with early signs of stabilization. Holding above $5.85 keeps the constructive structure intact, while clearing $6.20 is required to confirm a deeper push higher. Without this breakout, UNI remains vulnerable to pullbacks if broader sentiment weakens.

In earlier analysis, UNI was trading near $5.48 with persistent outflows, heavy EMA resistance overhead, and weak momentum near oversold territory. Today’s rebound marks the first meaningful structural improvement since that decline, but confirmation still requires reclaiming the $6.20 level.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Dogecoin Attempts Stabilization As Leverage Builds And Spot Flows Ease (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Dogecoin (DOGE-USD) is attempting to stabilize after a two-month slide that pushed the token to a low near $0.13, marking one of its deepest drawdowns of the year. The recovery toward $0.15 reflects tentative dip-buying interest, but the broader structure remains defined by a persistent downtrend channel and a market still unwinding excess leverage.

DOGE has not collapsed, yet the burden of proof has shifted firmly onto buyers. The daily chart continues to show DOGE respecting a clean descending channel, with overhead resistance between $0.158 and $0.165, where the mid-channel boundary intersects the 20-day EMA. A rejection here would keep downward momentum intact and risk a retest of the lower channel line near $0.125, a level that has repeatedly acted as a liquidity floor.

The more decisive battleground sits higher, between $0.171 and $0.18. This zone aligns with the 50-day EMA and a horizontal shelf that capped price action throughout early Q4. Until DOGE clears that area, rallies remain corrective rather than structural.

Yet the long-term accumulation base stretching back to March continues to hold. As long as the price stays above $0.128–$0.13, Dogecoin avoids structural damage and maintains the slow-build accumulation narrative that has underpinned its broader cycle.

A breakdown below that floor would shift the market toward deeper support at $0.105–$0.11, an area known for abrupt and volatile reversals when liquidity thins and shorts overextend.

Momentum indicators reflect this transitional phase. RSI sits near 45, rising from oversold territory but not yet signaling trend reversal. Historically, DOGE requires a breakout of the 55–60 RSI band to trigger sustained upside, a threshold the market has yet to approach.

Flows and positioning reveal a market repairing, not rallying

Spot flows show a market that has shed considerable risk. Repeated $40–100 million net outflows through September and October underscored investor reluctance to commit capital. The most recent print, however, shows a $620,000 inflow, the first positive reading in weeks. While small, it indicates the bleeding has slowed and sellers are no longer dominating every bounce.

The derivatives market tells a more complicated story. Open interest has risen 8.6 percent to $1.5 billion, with volume jumping 42 percent. Long/short ratios above 2.2 on major venues point to a heavily long-biased market, meaning the current bounce is driven by leverage rather than organic spot demand. If DOGE fails to break resistance, these leveraged longs risk liquidation cascades that could accelerate downside entirely through mechanical unwinds.

This dynamic is not unfamiliar. The last time DOGE saw rising open interest during a downtrend, the market was setting up for a washout that ultimately produced the late-Q1 rally. Rising leverage into weakness is historically fragile. A controlled squeeze lower could clear positioning and reset conditions for a healthier upside attempt - an outcome paradoxically favorable for medium-term bulls.

The short-term roadmap remains binary. A reclaim of $0.171–$0.18 would break the downtrend and open room toward $0.20–$0.21, where the 200-day EMA sits and where prior rallies failed. Conversely, a drop below $0.138–$0.14 would expose DOGE to a liquidity flush toward $0.115, a level where mean-reversion traders historically step in aggressively.

A market digesting, not collapsing

The deeper narrative surrounding DOGE is one of digestion rather than deterioration. The token is shedding speculative leverage accumulated earlier in the year while attempting to stabilize above long-term support. Spot selling has slowed, derivatives positioning is stretched but active, and sentiment remains hesitant but not despairing.

DOGE is now balanced between two competing forces: cheap enough to interest value-focused buyers, but not strong enough to force shorts to unwind. The next test of resistance will determine whether this bounce becomes a trend or fades into another liquidity event in a market still detoxing from its last cycle.

In earlier coverage, we noted that Dogecoin’s structure had entered a slow-grind corrective phase rather than a collapse, with long-term support levels showing resilience even as leverage unwound. The current price behavior aligns with that view. DOGE is still repairing, not reversing, and the key catalysts ahead remain its ability to reclaim EMAs and reverse the descending channel that has defined sentiment since early Q4.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: With Premium Eroded, I'm Diving In
data.description:
Though the stock market is rallying into year-end, there is plenty of evidence to suggest that investors' patience and risk appetite are waning, as clearly demonstrated by the sharp declines that many previously popular stocks have suffered in the Q3 earnings season.

Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM), the high-end grocer, is one of these stocks. After showcasing a surprising slowdown in comps sales growth and cutting its full-year outlook, shares of Sprouts have declined ~20% post-earnings, eliminating all the stock's year-to-date gains. Since January, Sprouts is now down ~35%, and the stock has also lost more than half its value relative to highs near $180 notched in May.

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on Sprouts in September when the stock was trading near $140 per share. At the time, I had cheered the company's market-beating comps growth, while also expressing hesitation about the stock's very sharp valuation premiums.

But for a value-conscious investor like me, the movement in Sprouts is a blessing. I'm more than willing to trade a bit of choppy growth trends in exchange for a lower, de-risked entry point. While acknowledging that Sprouts is facing the same consumer macro-driven headwinds that have hit stocks like Chipotle (CMG) and Cava (CAVA) when it comes to consumers choosing to "trade down" on their food baskets, I think Sprouts' long-term bull case still remains very much intact, especially as Sprouts continues to build up its margin profile even as sales growth slows. I'm upgrading the stock to a "Buy" rating.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this stock, here is my updated long-term bull case for Sprouts:

  • Taking market share in a staid grocery category. There's no doubt that Sprouts is off the peak of its growth rates. And yet, we note that mid-single-digit comps growth isn't a terrible result following a flat grocery industry, and the company is still also planning on increasing its store count at a high single-digit pace.
  • Secular tailwind in healthy eating. Sprouts boasts a unique selection of healthy products that aren't frequently found at other big-box grocery stores, which is why the company has appeal among higher-end consumers. Despite near-term macro headwinds, we think that Sprouts still benefits from the growing popularity of healthy eating and consumers spending more of their discretionary income on health.
  • Boosting structural tailwinds with higher gross margins. A favorable sales mix toward store brand products has been driving consistent gross margin expansion. The company is planning to release over 350 Sprouts-branded items in 2025 alone. Store brand sales are contributing to one-quarter of overall revenue, differentiating the chain further while also solidifying Sprouts' gross margin profile.
  • Building smaller stores to accelerate footprint expansion. The company is reducing average store sizes for its new builds to ~23k square feet (versus the ~30k original blueprint), which could help the company squeeze operating expenses while not sacrificing sales, and enable a faster pace of unit expansion as the company grows beyond its core coastal markets.

From a valuation standpoint, Sprouts now trades at a far more reasonable entry point, even against a slightly cut guidance outlook. At current share prices near $85, Sprouts trades at a market cap of $8.29 billion. After we net off the $322.4 million of cash and $53.4 million of debt on Sprouts' most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $8.02 billion.

For FY25, following a quarter of weaker comps in Q3, Sprouts has cut its full-year sales growth guidance to 14% y/y (previously 14.5%-16.0%), and comps to 7.0% y/y growth (from 7.5%-9.0% growth in the prior outlook). Investors have been critical of the fact that Sprouts is expecting very mild 0%-2% comps growth for Q4. Its EBIT guidance now stands at $675-$680 million, reflecting a 7.7% midpoint margin, essentially a $10 million cut at the high end versus a wider $675-$690 million range before.

For next year FY26, Wall Street expectations call for $5.76 in pro forma EPS (+9% y/y) on $9.69 billion in revenue, or 10% y/y growth. We note that considering Sprouts is currently growing its unit count at ~8% (37 net-new openings in FY25 versus ~460 total locations currently), it only takes low single-digit comps growth next year to hit ~10% overall sales growth, which I find to be a reasonable target. If we assume a further ~1-point improvement in adjusted EBIT margins to 8.7% (more conservatively set versus the company's current ~150bps pace of expansion), adjusted EBIT would be $843 million (+24% y/y).

This positions Sprouts' valuation multiples at:

  • 9.5x EV/FY26 EBIT.
  • 14.8x FY26 P/E.
  • 14.3x FY26 ex-cash P/E.

Within just a few months, Sprouts' rapid descent has given the stock a full round trip from being a premium growth stock to becoming a value stock. Given the number of long-term tailwinds that Sprouts enjoys as it expands its store footprint, I'd look past the near-term macro headwinds (which are largely priced into the stock already) and take advantage of the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 Recap

Let's now go through Sprouts' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Sprouts' revenue grew 13% y/y to $2.20 billion, missing Wall Street's expectations of $2.22 billion (+14% y/y) by one point, while also decelerating four points from 17% y/y growth in Q2. The most scrutinized data point, however, is the sharp deceleration in comps growth (which, as a reminder, measures revenue growth from existing stores and excludes the contribution of newly opened locations).

Comps in Q3 clocked in at 5.9% y/y growth, which is well below 10.2% growth in Q2 and 11.7% growth in Q1.

Management was pressed on the Q&A portion of the Q3 earnings call to explain the deceleration in comps. CEO Jack Sinclair chalked it up to both tougher macro headwinds and a tough prior-year comparison. What we find encouraging, however, is the company's insistence that the competitive/promotional landscape has not stiffened. Per Sinclair's remarks:

Well, I think the first thing we'd say is we're lapping some tough numbers from last year, and we may have underestimated the challenge of lapping those numbers. And it happened through the quarter as opposed to in the quarter. And as we look forward, we're anticipating a challenging environment. We know what we've got to lap going forward, and we're anticipating a little bit of pressure on the consumer going forward.

The health and wellness macro trends are still strong for us, and we see a real opportunity in doubling down on the loyalty program behind that going forward. And there are some macro pressures that I think you'll see coming through in the marketplace that we are certainly experiencing.

With regard to thinking about what should we do with -- we're not seeing a competitive dynamic changing dramatically in the marketplace in terms of pricing or activity. We've always promoted and we're very comfortable with promoting going forward. And we'll do what we need to do, but we're very strong in terms of managing our margins, managing our costs, and we'll consistently do that.

The comment on strong margin management and a lack of competitive dynamics/pricing shifts is well evidenced in Sprouts' 200bps y/y rise in gross margins to 38.7%. The company enjoyed a benefit here from a rising share of store brand products (which represent a quarter of overall sales), plus lower inventory shrink—all signs of healthy execution.

Overall EBIT, meanwhile, jumped 74% y/y to $147 million, representing a 7.2% margin that improved 150bps y/y. Again, we find Sprouts to be trading at an incredible bargain now relative to its earnings power: it's rare to find a stock in today's market that is growing its bottom line at >50% while also still trading at a single-digit multiple of EBIT.

Key Takeaways

To me, Sprouts' macro risks are clear, and it may take several quarters or even longer than a year until consumer confidence returns. But in my view, slower sales growth and category headwinds are already more than priced into Sprouts' stock. I avoided buying this stock when it was trading at excessive premiums, but I'm happy to accept near-term macro risk to invest in this name as a value stock. Take advantage of the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Stride's 60% Drop: A Broken Price, Not A Broken Business
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Over the last five years, Stride Inc.'s (LRN) performance was top tier in all financial aspects. But, after the management’s guidance below historical growth rates in Q1FY26 results, the company’s valuation saw 60% collapse in one month. I believe the long term story is still intact, and the fear in market is because of the temporary execution issue. And, this collapse might have created good entry points for the investors.

What is Stride?

Stride’s journey started as small technology-enabled education company in 2000. And, aim was simple: to expand high quality education for K12 students regardless of their geographic location. Since then, LRN has become one of the largest virtual public school program providers.

The main revenue source for stride is school-as-a-service contracts. Under these contracts, it takes care of everything from curriculum to administrative services for virtual and blended public schools.

Stride serves virtual public schools, charter schools, traditional school districts, private online schools and a small number of international schools. It also sells curriculum directly to families for homeschooling and supplemental use. The majority of its revenue comes from publicly funded schools and contributions of other types, but it is not as significant as public schools. Stride does not disclose the exact contribution of each, but public schools are certainly its main business.

What attracted me to Stride is its revenue contracts. Its majority revenue comes from multiyear school as a service contracts with public school governing boards & charter boards. These agreements' duration is generally more than 5 years and renews automatically if not cancelled.

Obviously, it won’t provide stable recurring revenue like a SaaS company which earns revenue multiyear subscriptions per consumer. And, the revenue levels can drop if the number of enrollments drops. School enrollments have declined slightly post-covid, but it has not affected stride’s business that much. And, to validate this statement, I checked public school enrollment data from the NCES. As per the latest available data, in 2023, total enrollments in public schools have reached 49.5 million which is almost 2.4% lower compared to 2019’s enrollment. But despite this decline, it has not impacted stride, and that is evident from the enrollment growth of stride in the last 5 years. This is enough to tell that gaining share within the public-school system.

The company does not explicitly disclose the revenue contribution of the homeschool business, but it is still relatively small. But, if we check homeschooling data, it could be a small growth factor for the company. Because the US Census Bureau shows homeschooling has risen from 3.3% of students in 2019 to over 11% in 2020 (this is normal because of covid), with levels stabilizing around 6% thereafter. It won’t be a significant growth engine for the company because most of the families depend on parent-led or low-cost materials rather than full-service providers. And, the main growth driver for the company is its ability to win share within the public school system.

I believe one more advantage of these long-term contracts is the high switching cost. After using stride’s services for multiple years, rebuilding or switching to a new LMS, curriculum library, and compliance frameworks is difficult.

Stride’s K-12 education has two parts. The first part is general education that covers core subjects such as English, math, science, etc. And, the second part is career learning, which educates middle and high school students in industry specific learning in which they want to pursue a career.

Career Learning is high margin business as compared to general education, and its revenue and enrollment growth are outpacing general education almost every year since FY2020. In FY2020, general education contributed 90% to the total revenue, but due to a slower growth rate compared to career learning, last year it contributed only 60% to the total revenue. General education’s contribution to total enrollments is at same level as revenue.

NCES data shows that there are 49 million to 50 million public K12 enrollments happening each year, and stride’s enrollments last year were around 0.234 million, so stride has captured around 0.47% of total enrollments. So, LRN still has huge market to penetrate.

Stride’s virtual public school is the largest service distribution channel. Other channels are traditional school districts adopting digital services and consumer programs.

How Was the Financial Journey?

Even though stride’s share price has melted almost 60% since its result, before evaluating its valuation we have to check its financials. Because without the healthy financials, hoping to get a sustainable valuation is foolish. I have created my own framework to evaluate financials with scoring.

Stride is not cyclical in nature like auto, steel, or consumer discretionary companies, and in the last decade it has shifted into a career learning business, which is high margin and fast growing business. Therefore, I am only analyzing the last 5 years' financial metrics.

After covid-19, families embraced digital learning, and career oriented education, this demand tailwind and Stride’s strong ability to retain its students became a revenue growth engine for the company in FY2020 to FY2025. In this period, 5 year revenue CAGR was about 18%. I know it can be inflated because of the sudden huge growth due to FY2020 lockdown. That is why I also calculated 4 year revenue CAGR, and even that was about 12%. With the revenue growth, the scale benefits were triggered. Also, Stride’s revenue mix shifted to high margin career programs. So, with the help of operating leverage and a high margin revenue mix, both gross margin and EBIDA margin climbed. EBITDA margin also benefited from the controlled SG&A proportion compared to revenue. Together, all these trends show the structural improvement in business rather than a one-off spike.

The strong margin growth also flowed down to the return ratios. However, they slipped in FY2023 due to flat profit growth and increased debt to fund M&A activities in FY2021. Increased return ratios are a sign of structural changes in business and disciplined capital allocation. Stride, an asset light business, can grow organically with low additional capital, and I believe that is the reason behind strong return ratios.

I believe the healthy financials are not limited to the income statement because if the income statement numbers are not flowing to the cashflow statement there is a high chance of manipulation.

With the meaningful margin improvement, the cashflow has also strengthened. This is a good sign because it shows that the income statement numbers are translating into cash with strong cashflow conversion ratios. FCF/EBITDA was mostly above 50% and I believe this is a healthy range for any asset-light business because it states that stride does not require heavy investment to scale its business. CFO/EBITDA was mostly above 0.9x. The only exception was FY2021, and even that was because of lagged payments. And, the good sign is that it normalized quickly next year and stayed above 0.9x FY2025. I believe healthy CFO/EBITDA and FCF/EBITDA in the last five years could be a sign that there is a healthy cash conversion without any manipulation. And, the cash conversion can support the scalability.

Even the balance sheet of the company is in a comfortable position. The debt to equity ratio is at 0.38x as per the Q1FY26 result, but in FY2022 it jumped to 0.7x because stride issued convertible notes in FY2021 to fund M&A activities.

But the good news is that the leverage has decreased continuously because of the faster growth rate of EBITDA compared to debt. The moderate range of debt to equity ratio and debt-to-EBITDA ratio is evidence of how well the company manage its balance sheet.

Now let’s check the liquidity. Cash & investments make up about 36% of the capital employed and about 122% of total debt. So, at the current stage the company has ample liquidity to meet the long term obligations.

We can say the same for the short term liquidity because, as per the last reported quarter, LRN’s current ratio is about 6.7x. And, it's not a one time jump because stride has maintained a current ratio of more than 3x since FY2022. Overall, Stride has a strong leverage profile and liquidity position.

Stride’s receivable days align perfectly with businesses which are dependent on the government. Accounts receivable turnover days of the company are in 70-90 days range, and it's very normal for a company like LRN because it is dependent on state payment schedules. However, the most important thing is that Stride has maintained accounts receivable as a percentage of sales tightly in the range of 23%-25% in FY2020 to FY2025.

The company has a strong financial history in all aspects, which can create strong foundation for investors. Now, we have to check whether these financials are sustainable going forward, and the bruised valuation can create an entry point for investors.

Long-Term Story is Still Intact

A 60% share price collapse in a month generally means the main growth story or something on a foundational level is broken. With stride, it was because of the muted growth guidance by management due to misfired platform upgrade. And, the management said that the platform integration hit caused them 10K to 15K enrollments and higher withdrawals. But, if you look at it from a fresh point of view, you will realize that this is not a structural issue which can hurt demand. The platform problem is an execution issue. The management itself has said that the issue will be resolved by the next financial year.

There is a high chance that the demand could be the highest because, currently, only 35% Americans are satisfied with the quality of the entire K-12 education which is predominantly the traditional, in-person public school system. And, this is the record low of the satisfaction rate. The unsatisfied parents can look for modern solutions like career learning services of strides for their kids. Because these services solve the curriculum irrelevance issue and specifically prepare students for the specialized career path they want to pursue. We can call these long term structural growth factors. Also, 44% parents fear the safety of their kids at school because of issues such as bullying and school safety. Even though 56% parents still feel safe about in-person schools, looking at the current penetration by Stride, even with 44% parents as potential, Stride has a huge addressable market.

Overall, the investor’s reaction is because of the near term headwinds, and long term story is still intact. To show trust in their company, the management announced $500 million stock repurchase program recently.

What’s the Company’s Real Value?

Right now, there is a lot of fear in the market about this stock & platform upgrade. So, rather than making my own assumptions, I will just work with the management’s current guidance to see if there is still any value remaining. So, the management’s FY2026 revenue guidance is $2.480-$2.555 billion, which is 5% revenue growth. The management has also given long term revenue guidance. They are forecasting that in FY2028, Stride's revenue will reach $3.3 billion, and they are still optimistic about achieving it.

We can assume that the long term guidance is intact because long term growth factors are still intact. And, management has said that the platform issue can cause low enrollments in the current year and result in 10k to 15k enrollment loss, which is about 4% to 6% of the FY2025’s total enrollment. To check how much it will actually impact the revenue, I have done a calculation in the table below. The in-year enrollment growth is impacted by the supply constraint not because of demand issues. To calculate FY2026’s revenue, I have assumed that revenue per enrollment can grow at 1.2% which is as per the statistics of the national center of education.

So, with the assumption that the long-term guidance is still intact, we can assume that the enrollment loss due to the platform issue will be recovered after the issue is resolved.

The company’s EBITDA margin has grown because of the structural change in revenue mix, and it is a permanent change. Even if the revenue mix stays the same and EBITDA margin stays the same as FY2025’s EBITDA margin in FY2027, we can expect the EBITDA of FY2027 to be about $564 million.

I have created three scenarios of EV/EBITDA multiples: current EV/EBITDA (Bear), EV/EBITDA before correction (Bull), and average (Base).

Even in the bear case scenario, the investors can earn about 7% annual returns. I believe this shows that the current valuation has a good margin of safety with attractive potential upside of 46% in two years or 21% annual returns.

Risk

The most important and near term risk Stride is facing is the upgrade of the new platform. The company’s financials and valuations are already hurt because of the failed upgrade most recently. If the upgrade fails again, I believe the repercussions could be much more severe because having bad technology for ed-tech can cause trust issues among users and damage the reputation.

Also, the overall online and blended K12 education market is crowded. Key competitors in the market are more well known players like Edgenuity (privately held) and a lot of small specialized curriculum and platform providers. This competitive pressure can capture the 10k-15K enrollment loss, which the company might lose due to a platform issue. However, if we look at the current market share, it is below 0.5%. So, even if the competitors capture these enrollments, with rising penetration, stride can fill out this loss quickly by increasing market penetration.

Another risk Stride faces is funding risk. Public school funding in the USA is dependent on state and local budgets. And, recent budget proposals are aiming to cut billions in K12 support. Because stride depends on public school contracts and the funding cuts can compress its revenue per student, and delay program approvals.

Conclusion

The long term story of Stride is still intact despite short term hurdles. The platform upgrade misfire has caused more damage than it deserves, and the underlying demand and financial strength of the company are still strong. Even though management’s guidance for FY2026 is below its historical growth, they are optimistic about the FY2028’s guidance. I believe the management’s long-term optimism makes my thesis even stronger. If the execution normalizes, we can see a rerating in valuation with 46% returns from the current market capitalization. The company’s financials are top tier as per my framework, and valuation has a margin of safety of 6% growth in the bear case. Even risk averse investors can allocate a meaningful (more than 10%) part of their portfolio to this stock.

data.title: 3 Potential Mid-Cap Biotech Buyout Targets In 2026
data.description:
The biotech sector got off to an inauspicious start to December in trading on Monday. The iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB) was off 2.14% yesterday while the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Biotech ETF (XBI) dropped 2.69% on Monday. This followed last week's big gains in a holiday trading week. The Dow was off .9%, the S&P 500 fell .53% and the NASDAQ pulled back .38%.

To be fair, it was a 'risk off' market decline. Small caps underperformed their larger brethren with the Russell 2000 declining 1.25%. Crypto continued to go deeper in its recent bear market with Bitcoin briefly diving below $85,000. To be fair, the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Biotech ETF was up 40% over the past four months, primarily due to a notable uptick in M&A industry across the industry. Some sort of pull back was overdue. That said, the biotech sector has stabilized in recent days and recovered Monday's declines.

In the past few months, many biotech names have been bought out for considerable buyout premiums since mid-September. Recently, we have seen two significant acquisitions of mid-cap biotech firms. In late October, Novartis (NVS) purchased Avidity Biosciences, Inc. (RNA) for a roughly $12 billion deal. Then in mid-November, Merck (MRK) took out Cidara Therapeutics (CDTX) for just over $220.00 per share in cash. This purchase was for approximately $9 billion, which included a bit over a 100% buyout premium.

So, which mid-cap biotech names could find themselves buyout targets in 2026? Three concerns I like as standalone entities but would also make logical acquisition targets are highlighted below.

We will start with Cytokinetics, Incorporated (CTYK), whose stock has had a very nice recent run, but is still trading under its levels in early 2024. The shares have climbed some 75% since last recommended in July. Currently the stock goes for around $65.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of just over $8 billion.

Cytokinetics primary asset is a candidate called aficamten which reduces excessive contractions in the heart. This drug is targeting hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or HCM. This disease impacts some 300,000 individuals in the United States annually.

Aficamten is tracking towards approval in the United States by the end of this year and approval in Europe should follow in the first half of 2026. Aficamten has shown strong clinical results and safety signals, so FDA approval is close to a foregone conclusion. Aficameten is partnered in Europe and China with drug giant Sanofi (SNY).

A recent article here on Seeking Alpha postulated that if Aficamten was approved for all the indications being evaluated within Cytokinetics' pipeline it could begin to approach the potential of AstraZeneca (AZN) Farxiga, which is close to $8 billion in annual sales. Cytokinetics should end 2025 with approximately $1.2 billion of cash on its balance sheet, so has funding in place for the marketing rollout and continued evaluation of Aficameten. Cytokinetics has been speculated as a potential buyout target of Amgen (AMGN) and Novartis (NVS) in the past. An article earlier this year, laid out the logic of a potential buyout.

Next up is Cogent Biosciences (COGT), which after a recent rally, trades near forty bucks a share and sport an approximate market capitalization of $6 billion. Cogent's main asset is a selective tyrosine kinase inhibitor {TKI}. It targets mutations within the KIT receptor tyrosine kinase. This TKI is called bezuclastinib. This candidate has significant potential. Both as a monotherapy, as well as part of a combination therapy.

On October 20th, bezuclastinib was granted FDA Breakthrough Therapy to treat NonAdvanced Systemic Mastocytosis or Non-AdvSM. Bezuclastinib is being evaluated to treat both AdvSM and non-AdvSM as a monotherapy in two late-stage studies. Leadership has projected the potential market for AdvSM in the $1.5 billion range. Top-line results from these studies should be out shortly.

The recent rally in the equity has been triggered by recent results from a trial testing combination therapy of bezuclastinib and Sutent (a current standard of care) from Pfizer (PFE) to treat a certain form of Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumors, or GIST. This data came out on November 10th and showed the combination therapy was much more effective than Sutent alone.

This news triggered a bevy of upward price target revisions from analyst firms including JP Morgan and Stifel Nicolaus. Management smartly leveraged this huge rise in its stock to do an additional capital raise on November 12th that brought in roughly $475 million in proceeds. This brought up the cash on its company's balance sheet up to some $850 million and puts Cogent in a good position to rollout bezuclastinib out when it is likely approved in 2026.

Management has positioned bezuclastinib as a potential alternative to Blueprint Medicines' (BPMT) Ayvakit. This PDGFRα/c-KIT inhibitor was approved in 2021 to treat AdvSM and did nearly $480 million in sales in 2024. It was a key reason Blueprint was purchased by French drug giant Sanofi in June of this year for just over $9 billion.

We conclude with a midcap biopharma company called Travere Therapeutics (TVTX), whose stock currently trades just under $35.00 a share with an approximate market capitalization of just over $3 billion. The stock has doubled since I gave it a 'thumbs up' as a covered call trade in May and this is how I still maintain holdings in this name.

Travere's main asset is a daily oral medication called Filspari. This compound received full FDA approval to treat IgA Nephropathy or IgAN in late summer of 2024. Filspari garnered approval in Europe and the U.K. in the spring of this year. It will be marketed and distributed on the continent by Travere's marketing partner there, CSL Vifor. Currently, Travere's marketing partner in Japan is evaluating Filspari in a Phase 3 registrational study. Of note, Filspari is the only non-immunosuppressive kidney-targeted therapy indicated for adult patients at risk of IgAN progression regardless of their proteinuria level.

Wedbush put Filspari peak sales for Filspari in the U.S. at just over $650 million when it was approved. Jefferies put peak revenues closer to $750 million. Travere is pursuing a label expansion for Filspari to treat focal segmental glomerulosclerosis for FSGS. This condition has no FDA approved treatments and could be a bigger market for Filspari than IgAN, which has several approved treatments.

The FDA stated in September than the sNDA around Filspari for FSGS would not require an AdCom Panel for its marketing application, triggering a nice rally in the stock. Filspari should garner FDA approval for this indication in mid-January. The analyst community is becoming more positive on the stock and there have been several upward price target revisions on TVTX in recent months (See Above).

Net product sales rose 85% on a year-over-year basis to $113.2 million in Q3. Travere should be increasing profitable and solid sales growth in coming years (above). The company's balance sheet is in good shape with nearly $300 million in pro forma cash against $316 million in convertible notes due in 2029.

Travere Therapeutics has not been the subject of significant buyout rumors to my knowledge. However, Travere would make an accretive acquisition for a larger biopharma name.

data.title: Costco's Earnings Setup: Tariff Roulette, Special Dividend Odds And A 50x P/E
data.description:
Costco's Earnings Approach

At this time of the year, investors usually look at retailers, trying to figure out which one could be the biggest beneficiary of the season's consumer spending trends.

As we are preparing for Costco Wholesale Corporation's (COST) earnings release on December 11th, we are hit but a sudden news that could have a meaningful impact on Costco's future results and that will probably determine the tone of the Q&A session during the earnings call.

Costco Sues The U.S. Government

We just learned that Costco sued the U.S. government "to secure full refund of tariffs." If we read the 17-page complaint that was filed on November 28, 2025, we see that the rationale of the lawsuit is double-sided.

First of all, it is pre-emptive, meaning that in case the Supreme Court rules against tariffs, any importer that already paid tariffs won't be automatically refunded. So, the lawsuit allows Costco to claim refunds, in case there is the opportunity to do so.

Secondly, the lawsuit highlights the urgency of the Supreme Court's ruling. In fact, on December 15, 2025, liquidations trigger. Costco asked the U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) to extend the deadlines while waiting for the landscape to become clearer, but it was denied. This implies that Costco has to pay its duties with the risk of seeing refunds barred.

As I showed many times in my previous coverage of the stock, Costco has a business model where it stores in its warehouses a few thousand different items, while other retailers, such as Walmart (WMT), have over 100k. This means Costco can work around tariffs with higher flexibility, by changing its merchandise mix and its sourcing. However, while Costco doesn't disclose the percentage of imported goods on its overall sales, some estimate that around one-third comes from outside the U.S, thus paying tariffs. Costco asks the court to declare unlawful the tariffs or, in case they are considered legal, to declare them unconstitutional because Congress can't delegate to the president to determine taxes.

Costco has usually been known for its low profile, politically speaking, which ensures it can usually bargain for the best possible deals for its members. While many will read the lawsuit from a political perspective, I think we should always keep in mind that the company's culture is member-oriented. From this point of view, I think the lawsuit shows the pressure that retailers have had to deal with since trade tariffs were implemented. If you have happened to read some earnings transcripts from the main U.S. retailers, it has been almost unanimously highlighted how consumers, though still spending, are increasingly cautious and value-oriented as they see prices go up on many imported goods. Now, if the lawsuit is successful, and Costco gets the refunds, both members and shareholders could probably see the company return that money to their pockets (a new special dividend? a membership refund?).

It is hard to say what the Supreme Court will decide. Surely, it will be a difficult task because the whole world is waiting for this ruling. However, we can see that betting on tariff refunds shows that the odds in favor of the Trump administration tanked in November after Supreme Court justices questioned the nature of the tariffs, with Kalshi giving a 29% chance and Polymarket assigning 25% odds in favor of tariffs.

While this is just a prediction, the betting market statistically gives accurate odds, and we can thus see that it shouldn't be excluded that the Supreme Court will declare the tariffs unlawful. We will see how it ends, since Treasury Secretary Bessent replied that even if the government loses the Supreme Court decision, the president will be able to use the 1962 Trade Act and recreate the tariffs.

Costco And The Tale Of Two Economies

As recalled above, this year we have observed that while the general consumer has kept spending, lower-income cohorts are feeling more and more pressure. This leads to what economists call a bifurcation: we see mid-to-higher income consumers continuing to visit stores, while the weaker cohorts are pulling back and reducing their trips to superstores, seeking value at discount stores. This is favorable for Costco. In fact, the company is widely known for its value proposition, where items are marked up by 14%-15% just to cover the fixed and variable costs linked to bringing them into a warehouse. The true profits for Costco come from the membership fee (we could truly assess the company only through the number of members it adds).

But Costco's members don't usually belong to lower-income tiers. After all, we know that once you visit Costco, your ticket will almost always be above $350-$400 (it might be fun to see in the comments what the average ticket at Costco is for SA readers). Higher-income households (with an income, more or less, above $125k) find Costco even more valuable right now because they have purchasing power, but at Costco, they see that their dollars can stretch further.

On the other hand, discount and dollar stores are seeing increased traffic from middle-income households that now budget more cautiously their spending. But this is a behavior that seeks dollar stores to buy less, rather than optimizing the purchasing power of the dollars a family wants to spend at a superstore.

As we can see below, the U.S. Consumer Health Index shows that lower-income earners, who are currently suffering from a softening labor market, are seeing their outlook score plunge to 95.6, representing a trend that started 6 months ago and that is the second longest ever recorded after the 9-month streak caused by the pandemic. Clearly, this group of people is revising downward their spending intentions, too (the score is now 94.7, with 100 being the neutral score). The result is that around 50% of U.S. consumer spending is actually coming from higher-income households, which obviously don't represent half of the population. This is what it means that the economy is splitting into two.

When we look at the most recent chain dominance data among superstores, we see that Costco has outperformed its peers, growing its visits by almost 10% in a year. Target (TGT), on the other hand, is the one that struggles the most.

This is even more important when we consider the graph below. We see that over the past year, discount and dollar stores grew 7.3%, followed by grocery stores (+5.3%), department stores (4.4%), and superstores (2.7%). This means that Costco truly outperformed the industry and took market share from its main peers.

Costco's Q1 2026 Earnings Preview

Costco always helps investors in preparing for the quarterly earnings release by issuing a monthly sales report that can help us monitor the company's performance quite regularly.

In October, the company reported that its monthly comparable sales were up 6.6% (ex-fuel and FX: +6.7%) in the U.S., with a MoM acceleration. However, this time Costco benefits from the negative impact that Hurricane Helen and the port strikes had in September 2024. Total sales for the month were $21.75B, which is an 8.6% increase YoY.

In November, Costco reported monthly sales of $23.64B, representing an increase of 8.1% YoY. We also learned the results for the first 12 weeks of the year: Costco reached sales of $65.98B, up 8.2% YoY. It is just remarkable how Costco keeps growing at a high single-digit rate even though it is massive in size.

Now, when we read the sales release, we receive a big help as analysts from the company. Costco is, in fact, a top-line company, and if we know its quarterly sales, it becomes much easier to predict what the quarterly report will look like.

With sales close to $66B, we have to consider the impact of the membership fees, which should be for this quarter $1.3B. This means that Costco will likely top estimates, which currently expect $67B in quarterly revenue. Costco's net income margin is usually 3%, which means the company will report $2B in net income. Costco has 443M shares outstanding, which means that the quarterly EPS should also beat estimates. With a 3% net income margin, Costco would report EPS of $4.51. Right now, the consensus expects $4.27, which means analysts expect tariffs to compress Costco's net income margin to 2.8%.

From this last sales report, I think the likelihood of Costco beating estimates increases.

The Special Dividend

There is also another big topic at stake. Costco is known for the special dividends it pays every two to three years. Its cash balance has rapidly increased from $10B to $14.2B in just one fiscal year. In December 2023, Costco paid a special dividend of $15.00, when its cash balance was $13.7B. Costco has 443M shares outstanding, which means that if it paid a $20 special dividend, it would return to its shareholders $8.86B, leaving Costco with something close to $5.5B to start 2026. So, we are above the threshold that could trigger another special dividend. If it won't be announced this year, I am willing to say that the odds of seeing it at the end of 2026 increase by a meaningful amount.

Even though I don't think the real bull case for Costco is its special dividend, it is a nice treat shareholders get. This could create a catalyst for the stock, which would surely attract some investors, just as we saw happening in late 2023. Perhaps, with the tariff issue that has not been settled, Costco will move with caution and postpone its dividend until it is clear if and how much it has to pay for import tariffs. I am inclined to think that the special dividend will be in 2026, but the setup has started to form.

Valuation

We know that when it comes to assessing the quality of the business, Costco has very few competitors. However, the real debate here doesn't focus on the company's quality or its prospects (Costco keeps growing at 8%-10% per year). The hot topic is its valuation. The stock soared and even traded for a bit above a 60 PE and a 40 EV/EBITDA. These multiples show two things, as far as I see it. Investors who buy Costco are willing to stay invested for the long run. Since valuation multiples basically express how long we are willing to wait to gain back our initial investment if the company's financials stay as they are at the time of our buy, here it is clear that Costco's shareholders have a multi-decade horizon.

But Costco soared in 2024 when investors expected a recession. In this case, Costco was a stock to play in advance. So far, we have not seen a recession, although, as we have said, the economy is splitting into two. So, this is why Costco's shares have gone nowhere this year and have actually let the multiple catch up a bit. However, we are still talking about a TTM PE of 50 and a fwd P/E of 45.

I think there is no way to solve the valuation riddle that hangs over the stock. There have been rare moments when the stock sold off. For example, I bought my shares when the stock was around $500, and the P/E was 35. I thought it was expensive back then, but I thought about its business 30 years into the future and thought I would still have a reasonable chance to make decent gains.

Right now, Costco is basically a stock that I would consider a "hand-down-to-your-heirs stock." The multiple implies a 4-decade horizon, which means that we pick it only if we truly consider it as part of the assets we want to give to our children and grandchildren. Otherwise, I would not suggest looking for a short-term trade. This is why I rate COST stock as a Hold.

data.title: Top Macro Trade For 2026: The Bet Against A Fed Hike
data.description:
Whatever happens, the Fed will not hike

I am working on my 2026 outlook for all asset classes and making initial positioning.

The main theme is that whatever happens in 2026, the Fed is unlikely to hike interest rates. My bet, as I will explain below, generates close to a 20% return - as long as the Fed does not hike in 2026.

First, here is the set up:

  • The Fed is expected to cut in December of 2025 down to 3.6%, and it signaled one additional cut in 2026 to 3.4% and an additional cut to 3.1% in 2027.
  • The market currently expected the Fed to cut in December 2025 and to cut twice in 2026 to 3.1%, with no hikes in 2027.

The Fed Chair Powell will be replaced in May 2026 by the new Trump-appointed Chair who will be "pushing" for more aggressive cuts, based on the current Fed Governor Miran's playbook. Whether the new Chair will be able to sway the FOMC to cut rates more aggressively (outside a recession) is questionable. However, it is very unlikely that the FOMC under the new dovish leadership will consider hiking in 2026, outside a super-major inflationary shock, which is very unlikely.

One of the top macro trades for 2026

So, this is one of my top macro trades for 2026: Short 3-month SOFR put option with expiration in December 2026, strike price 96.375. Current premium collected is $193.75, with a price of $0.0775 per one contract. The initial margin was $786 per option.

Thus, if the option expires worthless in December 2026, the rate of return would be around to 19% per each $1000 allocated to this trade, based on the current prices.

Here is the recent options chain for 3-month SOFR, expiring in December 2026.

Current at-the-money put option is 97 strike, which means that the market currently expects the Fed to cut interest rates down to 3% by December 2026 - and that's the most popular contract based on open interest. The bet that the Fed actually cuts down to 3% by selling 97 put would net a premium of $850, which means over an 80% rate of return per each $1000 allocated to the trade - this would be a very aggressive bet.

Note, the Fed signaled in the recent SEP that the target Federal Funds rate is 3.4% for 2026, and that would approximately translate to a December SOFR put with a strike at 96.625. If the Fed actually cuts one time in 2026 (considering a cut in December 2025), this option would net $375 to the seller. I am considering this bet; it has around 35% rate of return for each 1000 allocated to the trade.

If the Fed does not cut in 2026, and the December 2025 cut turns out to be the last cut of the normalization cycle, the 3-month SOFR would be approximately at the 96.375 strike price. The premium collected for this option is currently at $193. This is a more conservative bet with a close to 20% rate of return per $1000 allocated to the trade, and it is actually the most actively traded option. This is one of my top macro trades for 2026, based on the risk-return profile.

If the Fed does not cut in December 2025 and leaves the Federal Funds rate at the current level or cuts in December 2025 and then hikes one time in 2026, the 96.126 SOFR put would be at-the-money in December 2026, and the net premium collected by the seller is currently $106, or around 10% per $1000 allocated to the trade - this is the most conservative bet, which wins even if the Fed hikes in 2026.

Risk - what would force the Fed to hike?

So, what's the risk profile of the potential 20% trade, as described above?

If the Fed hikes in 2026, the December 97.375 put option would be in-the-money by approximately 25bpt, and the net loss would be approximately 18bpt after deducting the premium collected. With each basis point worth $25, the loss would be around $450 per contract. The maintenance margin for the 3-month SOFR contract decreases as the time expires, and it's around $250 for front end contact, so the trade would not get a margin call if $1000 is allocated for each trade, but the loss would be around 45% - if left unhedged. The market is currently giving around 5% probability of a hike in 2026, so that's the bet I am willing to take.

The 3-month SOFR generally closely tracks the Effective Federal Funds rate, unless there is a liquidity shock or a flight-to-quality shock, in which case the 3-month SOFR could rise above the Federal Funds rate - so that's the main risk.

In fact, we are currently in a liquidity crunch, as the chart below shows (difference between the 3-month SOFR and EFF). However, the liquidity crunch does not generally affect the distant-month futures; it affects mainly the front month contract.

Implications

The trade explained above is a macro trade, or a bet that the Fed will not increase interest rates in 2026. This analysis is part of my broader macro outlook, and I suspect that the financial market will be significantly affected by the Fed policy in 2026. The Fed is likely to turn more dovish in 2026, which could be bullish for the stock market (SP500) and other risk assets as bubbles continue to inflate. However, in my opinion, volatility will significantly increase, and the risk to the economy will be to the downside - which suggests even deeper cuts, and this would benefit the trade described in this analysis.

Note, selling naked options is a dangerous strategy, not recommended for most investors.

data.title: Rose's Income Garden November Raise: Verizon And Still A Buy
data.description:
November Income: 23 Payers

Rose’s Income Garden “RIG” portfolio has 78 investments and carries a 6.4% forward dividend income yield. Raises are very important to that income, and the higher, the better.

12 quarterly payments mark the end of the February, May, August, and November ('FMAN') sequence, with Verizon being the only raise received. The remainder were the 11 monthly high-yield payers discussed here in a recent article to get to 23 total for the month.

The most popular quarterly payment sequence is for the months of March, June, September, and December, which I call MJSD, with RIG having a total of 28 plus the monthlys to delightfully end December 2025. Therefore, when I do comparisons, I will only look from quarter to quarter. November was a low-paying month but was still slightly higher than its comparative payment month of August. The RIG current yield is 5.8%, and I will mention once again that it has a forward yield of 6.4%.

Review of Verizon and some competition

The chart below shows 4 communication sector companies that I actually owned this last year, showing each current dividend statistic and yield. I will reveal later the 2 sold, giving you a chance to guess which ones I sold.

Verizon Communications, Inc.

The company was formerly known as Bell Atlantic Corporation and changed its name to Verizon in June 2000. It was incorporated in 1983 and is headquartered in New York, New York. It has an S&P credit rating of BBB+. It provides wireless services and technology to businesses and private customers worldwide.

This was its 21st consecutive raise which was 1.25c from 67.75c to 69c which = 1.84%. It has a 5 year dividend growth rate of 1.98% as taken from FAST Graphs, a service to which I subscribe.

The raise at the current price gives it a yield of 6.8% and is also noted in the chart above.

The Chowder # which measures an investment purchase status by adding the 5 year dividend growth rate and yield is in this case 1.98 + 1.84 to = 8.78. An 8 is the suggested minimum # desired which it easily passes.

Seeking Alpha 1 year technical and price chart

Next I will show a 4 year FAST Graphs price and technical chart.

The black line is the price.

The dark green area represents earnings.

The blue line is the normal P/E line

The yellow line represents the dividend and shows it is well covered by earnings.

Dotted lines and any E behind a number represent estimates.

The FAST Graph shows the current P/E to be 8.67x and the normal 8.36x.

It would seem, using the P/E, the price is just a bit above normal.

Rose Recommendation

Most of the metrics presented continue to point to Verizon being a quality, reliable, and nice high yielding company. It is very buyable if you want a great income stock and don’t own it. I do own it and enjoy it very much, but watch for a 7% yield to add on for me once again.

3 Competitors

1- BCE Inc.

This telecommunications sector company provides wireless, wire line, internet, streaming services, and television (TV) services to residential, business, and wholesale customers in Canada. It was founded in 1880 and is headquartered in Verdun, QC.

The very sad news from BCE is that it cut its dividend this year down to 32c from ~69c. The price has compensated for that cut to lower, which now gives it a nice yield of ~5.4%. I have held on and will continue to do so but will watch it closely. I do believe cutting the dividend was the right management move. It does have an ADR fee, and dividends may vary by the foreign exchange rate.

2- ATT Inc.

This company is well known and has been around with different names for years. The company was formerly known as SBC Communications Inc. and changed its name to AT&T Inc. in 2005. AT&T Inc. was incorporated in 1983 and is based in Dallas, Texas, and has an S&P credit rating of BBB.

If you guessed I sold this one, you were right. It has not raised the dividend for many years now, and it probably will remain as such for a few more. This was an excellent income stock, but now, sadly, it does not provide the yield of almost 6-7% it had so many years ago. I sold T twice this past year at $28 and $29.06 and don’t regret it, as it has done just what I thought it would do, and the yield remains low.

3- Vodafone Group Public Limited Company

Lastly, this is the other one I sold. Vodafone offers services in Germany, the United Kingdom, much of Europe, Turkey, and South Africa. It was incorporated in 1984 and is based in Newbury, the United Kingdom, and has an S&P credit rating of BBB. It pays dividends twice yearly and most recently cut them. The next installment is 23.9c to be paid February 5, 2026. (Don’t hurry to get it if you want it, as the ex-date was Nov. 24, 2025). It also has an ADR fee.

Below is the 5 year SA chart to show its decline over the past years.

I sold this in August 2025 for $11.38 and had a yield then of 4.2%. The price is slowly rising up a bit, making the yield actually lower now and under 4%. The SA chart is not correct with the dividend rate, so the yield is correct in my chart above for 3.88%. I enjoyed ~6% before the cut, but management did do the right thing by cutting the dividend. I never enjoyed the twice a year dividends and strange ex-dates, so I have no regrets about selling it other than it took me too long to do it.

Summary/Conclusion

Having dividend cuts in a portfolio is not advantageous, and I certainly hope I have learned the lesson. I will continue to look for companies with quality cash flows into the future. I did learn S&P credit ratings may be kept if a company cuts the dividend to maintain a lower payout, and it alone is not exactly a sure investment metric to rely on. RIG continues to do well and is up 13.3% YTD, and as mentioned, income has been quite reliable at 5.8% this year.
